sentences.text: I am Henry Wolfgang Meyer.
My name is spelled M-E-Y-E-R.
I used to be Heinz Meyer, H-E-I-N-Z.
And I was very often called Heinzy.
This originated from the youth group I was in which had five Heinzs, and we had to find some way out.
So I got Heinzy.
I was born in Dresden on the 29th of June, 1923.
Dresden is spelled D-R-E-S-D-E-N, and has been in the eastern part of Germany, being close to the Czechoslovakian border.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was born, as I said before, in 1923.
My parents were upper middle class, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but that only should show the financial end of it.
They were very bright people who were very concerned about the education of my brother and me.
They both were amateur musicians.
So my mother was the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] head of the stores which my parents had.
My father married into the business.
He came from Oberschlesien, as its called, a few miles from Auschwitz, where I finally ended up.
The life in Dresden was very, very pleasant.
It was a very beautiful city, very cultured city.
It had everything you could think of-- famous opera house, many concerts, theaters, museum.
And really, one could not wish for more.
We sort of had a very normal life, which perhaps changed only at the moment where it was discovered that I had a special talent for music, and when I started to learn the violin.
My father used to be an amateur violinist and played extremely well for somebody who never-- hardly ever had any instructions.
My mother was an excellent pianist, but she was trained.
And my parents played a lot together, I would say practically every evening, sonatas, when they didn't go out for concerts and other things.
Pardon me?
Oh, my parents' names.
My father was Harry Herbert Meyer.
He had an American English name, Harry, from birth.
And probably would have been very happy to use it at another time, but it didn't come to it.
My mother was Johanne, Johanne, born Schifter, S-C-H-I-F-T-E-R.
She was born near Dresden, in a little place which is next to the little bit bigger city Potschappel, P-O-T-S-C-H-A-P-P-E-L.
And it is close to Radebeul, R-A-D-E-B-E-U-L.
My grandparents came from Romania and settled near Dresden in Potschappel, where they had their first store.
My grandfather branched out and had then three stores in Dresden.
And they were rather known.
They were sort of small department stores with a special emphasis on toys and leather goods.
[GERMAN] was the biggest of the one.
And it was on the [GERMAN], which was then the center of the town.
Today, it looks a little bit different.
As all of that, the old part of the city was bombed and is not reconstructed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My parents lived a very active life, had many friends.
And then, one day, it must have been 1929, my father came back from the fair in Leipzig, where my parents always bought whatever was needed for the stores, and brought along a violin for me.
It must have been a half-sized fiddle.
And I was registered at the conservatory and started to learn the violin.
Now, that went very, very fast, because, at the age of eight, I played already with the Dresden Philharmonic, and as a soloist in one of the Sunday concerts.
I played with the Orchestra of the Mozart [GERMAN], and so on.
And to put that in perspective is that 1933, my solo career was already finished because Hitler came into power and I had to restrict my playing to a Jewish organization.
My brother followed suit.
When he was six years old, he started to play the piano, in a different way than I did.
My brother always did only what he liked to do.
And the rest, you could stand on your head, he didn't show any interest and let it go.
He was a young brother, two years younger.
His name was Fritz Joachim Meyer, F-R-I-T-Z, Joachim, J-O-A-C-H-I-M.
From this moment on, my mother had a hard time to come close to the piano again, because he dominated.
And he was-- I am not a good judge, but I think he was the greater of the two talents of us.
We had plenty of talents.
It went very fast.

I know that, after about a year at the conservatory-- the young violin teacher was a lovely man by the name of Slovinsky, S-L-O-V-I-N-S-K-Y-- gave me a copy of the Bach Solo Sonatas and Partitas, and said, I'm sure you will play them one day, and you'll play them well, but I cannot teach you anything anymore.
You need a better teacher.
Well, I have been a teacher for nearly 50 years.
I have never heard such an understatement from anybody.
Anyway, my father saw to it that I got another very good teacher.
And my parents always saw to it that we got the best there could be.
For instance, every Sunday, my father had to go and wind up the light clocks.
So in the evening, the light would go on in the display windows.
That, he did in the following fashion.
We would leave the house at 9:30 in the morning.
We would go to one of the most beautiful museum in Dresden, only in one hall, never more, and discuss the pictures in our way.
Sometimes, very funny comments.
Then we went on to the [GERMAN].
The reason for it was that, at 11 o'clock, there was a high mass, and the orchestra of the opera played, and the soloists were the singers from the opera.
So I am very familiar with masses by Schubert, Hayden, and Mozart.
And then, we went on to our stores.
My father would wind the clocks, and we went back home to have dinner.
Sunday, the warm meal is eaten at noon.
When we grew up and had our own activities, my father was very, very unhappy.
And we had to fight for every free Sunday because he felt cheated not to be able to go with us.
But it was a unique way of being educated.
I returned to Dresden and looked at the museum and the Gemaldegalerie, and I felt totally at home.
So pictures were not at the same places, but the pictures, as such, are engraved in my memory, and I know quite well.
Needless to say, when I am all over the world, teaching and doing things, I do go to museums.
And it's very fortunate with the career of our quartet, that I have seen very many and that we played in very many of them.
Well, let's go back.
We went to normal schools.
We were good students.
School didn't hamper me at all.
I was always at the top of the class.
There was one subject I was at the bottom of the class, and that was physical education.
And this was not my strength.
But that was not so important.
My brother was similar, except with the one matter which I said before, that he just failed in many subjects because he wasn't interested.
He was equally talented in mathematics and also performed that in a peculiar way.
He did everything in his head and refused to write the way you should do it down.
He only wrote the question, answer, finished.
And he was forced by teachers.
He was accused of cheating.
He said, no, no.
Try another time and I'll show you.
That's what they finally had to do.
We played together quite a bit.
And it was a great pleasure.
And we also performed wherever it was possible in Jewish circles.
We then went to Berlin at a certain time, my brother and myself, because there was still a Jewish music school.
But that came at a later date.
Pretty soon, it turned out that a number of our teachers couldn't teach us anymore, I mean our musical teachers, that the Gestapo was after them.
I was very lucky, because, at the age of 14, I was sent to Prague, to Czechoslovakia, which, then, was just fantastic country.
The city of Prague, very, very beautiful, still today.
And very many of the Jewish people were very interested in music, especially chamber music.
And I played on a Sunday from 10 o'clock in the morning until midnight.
Going from one group to other, being very popular.
After all, there were not so many 15 year old one.
And my general education wasn't really neglected.
I was enrolled at the music school, the German music school in Prague, school with a wonderful reputation.
I was too young to be in the regular program, so I was a special student, and took the subjects I liked.
And I had a very good time.
But what about all the other things?
I was kicked out of school when I was 13 years old.
And I have not been in a regular school.
You were kicked out of school because you were Jewish?
Jewish, yes.
No, otherwise, I made it.
And when I came to Prague, I was housed with a family, Emil Factor.
He used to be the chief editor of the B-O-R-S-E-N, an equivalent of the or The very amazing part is that this man was the editor, being a poet and a writer.
You don't find that very often.
Being Jewish, he had to leave Berlin in 1933 and returned to the city of his birth, and tried to raise a family there with two children.
It was very difficult.
He was the editor of a very mediocre noon hour newspaper and went to-- every Friday, new movies were shown.
So the entire family went to movies.
And we would meet at tonight in a restaurant or something and reported how we liked the movie.
And then, he would write it up the next day, only to make a few bucks.
But he was an extremely intelligent and learned man.
He liked me very much.
And there was a library, which was absolutely fantastic.
And I was asked to read certain books and then report about them.
Wonderful way of being taught.
His wife used to be a concert pianist.
And in my room, there was a big Bechstein piano.
And I played either four hands with her, or she accompanied me in Mozart's sonatas, Beethoven's sonata.
I must say, and I will say it a little bit more often during this-- during this interview, I must have been born under a lucky star.
The only thing left today is that, when I go downtown, I find a parking place right away.
But, otherwise, I am really-- there were so many lucky breaks, it's amazing.
Well, this was, of course, wonderful.
I had everything I wanted.
Besides that they were very respected citizens.
I was taken to first performances, to gallery openings, to everything.
This was all at the age of 15, without having any-- Besides that, they had a very nice daughter.
That was another plus.
She is now living in Berlin, being married to Ossip Flechtheim, a futurologist at the University in Berlin.
They returned from America to Berlin.
They are very, very old and not very healthy people.
But I never fail to see them.
And she is the only survivor of the family.
The parents, Factor, and her brother all disappeared, and nobody really knows how and where to.
That was my stay in Prague.
By one coincident, unlucky coincident, I was assigned to play a concert in Dresden around the beginning of November 1938.
Well, the 9th of November was the Crystal Night.
And I just had arrived in Dresden.
I can't remember any more what date it was and when my concert was.
Anyway, my concert would have been in the synagogue or the annex of the synagogue.
And it never took place.
My father was warned by good-natured, decent Gentile friends that there would be a spontaneous uprising the next night.
And he should get out of the way.
And he did.
And the Gestapo came to get him.
I wasn't there.
My grandmother was the only person in the house.
My father's mother lived with us.
And the Gestapo started to push her around.
I was sort of hidden.
We had a big apartment then.
And I showed myself.
They said, well, who's that.
And she said, why, that's my grandchild.
He's very young.
Well, there was one difference, of course.
I came from Prague.
And in Prague, you had decent slacks and things like that when we were 15.
In Germany, the Hitler Youth, the young boys were wearing short pants, which put any mini-skirt to shame.
It was so short.
So, of course, that makes you look a little bit younger.
And I looked a little bit older.
So they said, he will do.
And they took me along.
My father was informed by somebody of the family, we knew where he was, and went to the police department and gave himself up, with the hope that they would release me.
Nothing of the sort.
My father had to be released.
He was a veteran, a decorated veteran, and they had to release him.
And I was sent to Buchenwald.
I didn't stay too long.
Stay too long?
One day is too long.
But I got out again because my parents were able to start procedures for immigration to another country.
Well, that didn't go very far.
I got out of Buchenwald and returned to Dresden, and was allowed--
I worked, at first, for the Jewish congregation in Dresden, and then was allowed to go to Berlin and study some more at this Jewish music school.
And the immigration-- I don't know anymore which one that was, I think trying to come to the United States.
But as we thought we could do it all, the entire family together, it immediately was terrible.
Our quota was-- the number of the quota was way, way, way, from the time we lived there, it would have been two or three years later.
But my father, who was born in a place which always fluctuated between Germany and Poland then happened to be at the Polish quota.
And that would have been something like 25 years.
So it would have meant, when we come up, we three would go and my father would stay behind.
And that was not acceptable.
So actually, as grotesque as it is, we were happy that we couldn't go either.
Because we wanted to go as a whole family.
All right, I went to Berlin.
My brother also went to Berlin.
And we stayed privately, as always.
And we had a wonderful time in Berlin, except I wasn't very satisfied with my violin teacher.
I have to be fair.
I don't even know if he was good or bad.
I wanted to study with another one.
And you know how you cut out one end of it.
The other one that was Max [GERMAN], then called Mordecai Finkelstein.
And he was a concert master of the Jewish [GERMAN] Orchestra.
By coincidence, he lived around the corner in Berlin.
And I always ended up on the same street car.
So one day, I took all my courage together, and I said I would like to play for you.
He said, well, come this afternoon.
And I did.
Oh, is it very nice?
Sure, accept you as a student, but you should play in the orchestra.
I said, fine.
Come tomorrow morning at 10:00, and we are going to rehearsal.
I will introduce you to the then music director of the orchestra, Rudolph Schwartz, a very wonderful man who went through plenty.
Well, he can't tell his story anymore.
He died last year.
And I played for him at 10 o'clock the next morning.
And I played the first movement of the Mozart concerto.
And he accompanied me.
And he said, wow, you come right away with me, we have rehearsal.
So we went down to the rehearsal.
And the only seat in the section at that moment was right next to my new teacher, which meant the assistant concert master.
So Schwartz had a peculiar way of rehearsing his orchestra, especially as strings were concerned.
You had to play difficult passages yourself.
You were absolutely exposed.
The piece we were playing was Scheherazade by Rimskey-Korsakov.
And that's a very, very difficult first violin part.
I had never seen the piece.
I had never heard it.
And there I was.
The first difficult place came up, and Schwartz said, everybody alone.
And I thought, fine, I just came he's not going to ask me.
He said, Mr.
Meyer, I don't know what he said, Heinz.
So there I was.
The only thing I saw at the moment that all figures behind the music stand sort of came up and looked, what will he do.
Well--
